paragraphs.text: Dear commission, I am writing a two-part letter to bring to your attention several aspects of digital goods ownership that have not been addressed yet in your initiative, while being of high value to the gaming community as well. Due to the scope of the issues and the lenght limit, I cannot provide in full detail on the matter here. The first part mainly addresses four issues related to what happens after the sale of goods is completed and what rights and legal protections the customers have, do not have, and should have. The concerns tackle shared issues across all digital media, not only limited to the video games. 1. Piracy in the light of abandonware Over the decades developers and publishers have abandoned many digital media [like games/software/movies], their support and pulled those digital media permanently from sale, to the point there are no legal, official ways of acquiring them to the viewers' enjoyment. In such cases, if a given digital medium is not legitly or easily [purposely obstructed] available in one's region [after a certain set period since it became unavailable], the customer should have a legal protection and a right to resort to the piracy, to share and download, as today this is in many cases the only way of experiencing those [effectively] lost media. The fact that a developer/publisher no longer provides a legal way of purchasing the digital media, indicates they have no intention or ability to continue to profit from the game, and the right to piracy would not negatively impact any potential profit they could make. The so-called in the marketing as "Enhanced Editions/Remasters/Remakes" should not be a valid substitute of the original work that became lost to time. 2. Version archival for personal use As majority of digital media today is shared, bought and downloaded via the internet from the publisher's hosting service, they are a subject of forced automatic updates, which may introduce security flaws, replaced content, removed features, and alike. In those events, users and customers of said digital media have no choice but to update their possession to the latest version, even if it is flawed or inferior. While it is unreasonable to expect the publishers to provide every single version of their digital media in existence, the customer should have a legal protection and a right to make back-ups of their owned version and use it unobstructed, ideally the publishers should provide an option to downgrade the digital media to the previous stable version. 3. Emulation of retired console systems We have a history of multiple different generations of consoles and their systems that have been long out of service and sale. For anyone new, who is interested in experiencing any of the digital media made for those systems, the only effective way is through the use of system emulators. Owners of those retired systems should have a legal protection and a right to create and share emulators, and new users should have a legal protection and a right to use those emulators. 4. Game modifications and fan content Cultural pieces often create dedicated communities of fans around them, many of which use their skill to celebrate those pieces by creating non-profit bug-fixes, game modifications, fan games and other types of derivative artistic expressions. However some of the biggest and most influential companies actively fight against their own fans and the much-valued art they create. Notorious examples of disrespect towards fans and their content include the "Nintendo" and "Rockstar Games/Take-Two Interactive" companies, thanks to who countless art projects have been forcefully shut and lost. Owners should have a legal protection and a right to modify, derive and share game modifications, fan games, or any other sort of artistic fan expressions, as long it is non-profit [unless agreed otherwise with the IP owner] and does not distribute for free the original work.
